coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive detection of Campylobacter jejuni using a carbon dot-embedded nanoMIPs fluorescent sensor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Dinç, Saliha
Tjardts, Tim
Maschkowitz, Gregor
Lukaszczuk, Vivian
Taghi Gharibzahedi, Seyed Mohammad
Altintas, Zeynep
coredata.dc:description: Campylobacter jejuni, a leading cause of gastroenteritis worldwide, poses significant detection challenges due to the labor-intensive and insufficiently sensitive current methods. Developing efficient, rapid diagnostics is vital for clinical and food industry applications; yet the vulnerability of biological elements in creating bacterial sensors remains a major obstacle. This study introduces an innovative fluorescence-based sensor employing fully synthetic carbon dot (CDs) functionalized molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs@nanoMIPs) receptors to detect this foodborne pathogenic bacterium. The CDs@nanoMIPs synthesis was accomplished using a solid-phase approach, with an immunodominant epitope of C. jejuni serving as a guiding template. During the polymerization process, nitrogen-doped CDs were synthesized in situ and incorporated into nanoMIPs as a fluorescent tag, constituting approximately 19 % of the composite. The synthesized nanomaterials were characterized by employing transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), dynamic light scattering (DLS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), fluorescence microscopy, UV–Vis spectrophotometry, and fluorescence spectrometry. CDs@nanoMIPs demonstrated a notable blue fluorescence when excited at 350 nm, along with excellent photostability and a negative zeta potential. These nanostructured particles (59 nm) exhibited moderate polydispersity and a spherical morphology. The FT-IR spectrum deviated from that of conventional CDs, aligning more closely with the template epitope. When in contact with C. jejuni, CDs@nanoMIPs induced a significant increase in fluorescence intensity, enabling the efficient bacterial detection. This interaction showed exceptional affinity and sensitivity towards C. jejuni, featuring a linear range of 1 × 101–1 × 108 CFU mL−1 (R2 = 0.98) and a low detection limit of 4.6 CFU mL−1. CDs@nanoMIPs-based C. jejuni sensors marked a novel approach to pathogen detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Campylobacter jejuni
Flagellar capping protein
Carbon dots (CDs)
CD-embedded nanoMIPs
Fluorescent sensor

Selection of specific epitope for C. jejuni
Characterization of N-doped CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs
Optical properties of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs
Morphology and size of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs
Surface functional groups of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs
Selectivity of the proposed fluorescence sensor
Specificity of the proposed fluorescence sensor
Detection of C. Jejuni in tap water

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support provided by the German Research Foundation (DFG, Grant number: 428780268) and the Aventis Foundation (Grant number: 80304368). Also, the authors would like to appreciate Justin Jetter (M.Sc. Institute of Materials Science, Kiel University) for conducting XRD analysis and the Scientific and Technical Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK) for providing a scholarship (grant number: 1059B192000595).

The authors gratefully acknowledge the support provided by the German Research Foundation (DFG, Grant number: 428780268 ) and the Aventis Foundation (Grant number: 80304368 ). Also, the authors would like to appreciate Justin Jetter (M.Sc., Institute of Materials Science , Kiel University) for conducting XRD analysis and the Scientific and Technical Research Council of Turkey ( TUBITAK ) for providing a scholarship (grant number: 1059B192000595 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis of CDs@nanoMIPs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs. Insets: photographs of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs taken under 350 nm. (B) Normalized fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs excited under 345 nm. (C) Excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of CDs. (D) Emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (E, F) Emission spectra of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs at different concentrations. (G) Photostability graphs of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs under UV excitation. (H) TEM image and the size distribution histogram of CDs@nanoMIPs.
(A) XRD pattern of CDs. (B) The hydrodynamic size distributions of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs. (C) Fluorescence microscopy image of CDs@nanoMIPs. (D) Zeta potential profiles of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs.

High-resolution XPS spectra of C1 and N1 in CDs, along with an elemental content comparison between (A) CDs and (B) CDs@nanoMIPs. (C) XPS and (D) FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs. The FT-IR spectrum of template epitope is shown in (D).

(A) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence detection of C. jejuni using CDs@nanoMIPs. (B) Calibration curve showing the sensor's response to template epitope concentration. (C) Calibration curve showing the sensor's response to C. jejuni count. (D) Selectivity of the proposed biosensor with control-CDs@nanoMIPs against C. jejuni concentration. (E) Specificity of the fluorescent sensor for detecting C. jejuni at various concentrations in comparison to non-specific bacteria. (F) Calibration curve of the sensor's response to different concentrations of C. jejuni in tap water.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive detection of Campylobacter jejuni using a carbon dot-embedded nanoMIPs fluorescent sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Kiel Nano, Surface, and Interface Science (KINSIS), Kiel University, 24118 Kiel, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Kiel Nano, Surface, and Interface Science (KINSIS), Kiel University, 24118 Kiel, Germany

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Materials Science, Faculty of Engineering, Kiel University, 24143 Kiel, Germany.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: Çumra School of Applied Sciences, Selçuk University, 42500 Konya, Turkey.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Campylobacter jejuni, a leading cause of gastroenteritis worldwide, poses significant detection challenges due to the labor-intensive and insufficiently sensitive current methods. Developing efficient, rapid diagnostics is vital for clinical and food industry applications; yet the vulnerability of biological elements in creating bacterial sensors remains a major obstacle. This study introduces an innovative fluorescence-based sensor employing fully synthetic carbon dot (CDs) functionalized molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs@nanoMIPs) receptors to detect this foodborne pathogenic bacterium. The CDs@nanoMIPs synthesis was accomplished using a solid-phase approach, with an immunodominant epitope of C. jejuni serving as a guiding template. During the polymerization process, nitrogen-doped CDs were synthesized in situ and incorporated into nanoMIPs as a fluorescent tag, constituting approximately 19 % of the composite. The synthesized nanomaterials were characterized by employing transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), dynamic light scattering (DLS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), fluorescence microscopy, UV–Vis spectrophotometry, and fluorescence spectrometry. CDs@nanoMIPs demonstrated a notable blue fluorescence when excited at 350 nm, along with excellent photostability and a negative zeta potential. These nanostructured particles (59 nm) exhibited moderate polydispersity and a spherical morphology. The FT-IR spectrum deviated from that of conventional CDs, aligning more closely with the template epitope. When in contact with C. jejuni, CDs@nanoMIPs induced a significant increase in fluorescence intensity, enabling the efficient bacterial detection. This interaction showed exceptional affinity and sensitivity towards C. jejuni, featuring a linear range of 1 × 101–1 × 108 CFU mL−1 (R2 = 0.98) and a low detection limit of 4.6 CFU mL−1. CDs@nanoMIPs-based C. jejuni sensors marked a novel approach to pathogen detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Campylobacteriosis, a widespread form of gastroenteritis globally, is mainly caused by the zoonotic pathogen Campylobacter. Among the strains of Campylobacter, C. jejuni is a major cause of human infections, affecting around 550 million individuals annually, including children under five [1]. In recent years, there has been a significant rise in campylobacteriosis cases [2]. C. jejuni, found in poultry and wild birds, is the main culprit for this illness. The disease symptoms, such as diarrhea, abdominal cramping, and fever, are usually mild and resolve within 1–2 weeks. Specific vulnerable populations, such as the elderly, immunosuppressed individuals, and children under two years old, face a higher risk of severe outcomes like fatality [2,3].

Considering the notable prevalence, extended duration and possible post-infection complications, the prevention of C. jejuni infections is of utmost significance from both socio-economic and public health standpoints. Hence, the rapid and reliable detection of C. jejuni in food products is essential through efficient diagnostic methods to facilitate routine food surveillance, screening, and identification to reduce infection risks [4]. Rapid techniques like polymerase chain reaction (PCR), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and nucleic acid-based molecular biology methods offer shorter timeframes compared to traditional culture methods. However, these methods still pose challenges in terms of on-site usability, require skilled personnel, and are associated with high costs and labor-intensive processes [5]. As an alternative, a diverse range of sensors that make use of nanoparticles, including gold nanoparticles and quantum dots (QDs), in conjunction with recognition elements like DNA, antibodies, aptamers, specific proteins, and more, have emerged. These sensors, operating through optical, electrochemical, and quartz crystal microbalance platforms, have convincingly demonstrated their capability for real-time C. jejuni detection [4–9]. This innovative strategy effectively leverages the unique properties of nanoparticles to overcome the limitations associated with traditional culture methods. Although some studies have reported very low limits of detection (LODs) for C. jejuni using fluorescence immunosensors [5,7,10], these values may still fall short of the requirements in the food industry and clinical applications, considering that C. jejuni-induced infections can occur even at low concentrations. Hence, ongoing research efforts are crucial to develop new sensors that better meet the necessary criteria. Moreover, the use of aptasensors and immunosensors comes with challenges, including the labor-intensive handling of antibodies, their high cost, and limited shelf life [11]. The pursuit of accurate and efficient molecular recognition remains a pivotal challenge in sensor development, mainly due to the lack of specific receptors customized for C. jejuni detection [12].

The emergence of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), often dubbed synthetic or artificial antibodies, takes center stage in sensor innovation. When designed and developed properly, MIPs provide exceptional selectivity and sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, robust chemical and thermal stability, facile functionalization, and remarkable performance and storage stability [13]. However, achieving effective imprinting of microorganisms (e.g., bacteria or viruses) and macromolecules (e.g., proteins), poses significant challenges due to their high molecular weight, intricate structure, conformational variability, and susceptibility to imprinting conditions [14]. Considering these challenges, the concept of epitope imprinting, inspired by the lock-key mechanism, presents a promising approach to crafting custom artificial receptors designed for bacterial detection [15]. Using the solid-phase synthesis method, which involves polymerization around a covalently immobilized template on a suitable solid support, allows for the creation of MIP nanoparticles (nanoMIPs). These nanoMIPs demonstrate monospecific binding properties, displaying remarkable sensitivity, selectivity, and rapid analyte recognition in biological samples [16]. Like antibodies, the solid-phase epitope imprinting approach produces uniform nanoMIPs with well-defined high-affinity binding sites, delivering advantages in medical diagnosis and therapy [17]. In the context of pathogen detection, fluorescent sensors have gained substantial attention, including their application to pathogens like C. jejuni. This is due to their many advantages, including heightened sensitivity, rapid response times, very low LODs, chip- and surface chemistry-free detection principles, and enhanced accuracy [7,10,18]. CDs have been gaining more attention in the field of fluorescent sensors since their discovery. This is due to their remarkable optical properties, high water solubility, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, and ease of synthesis [19]. Nonetheless, the integration of CDs with nanoMIPs (CDs@nanoMIPs) remains a relatively unexplored and underreported research area.

In this study, we introduce a new method to develop a fluorescent sensor based on CDs@nanoMIPs for the specific detection of C. jejuni. The process began with the synthesis of nitrogen-doped CDs (N-doped CDs) as a unique fluorescent tag. Afterward, the amino group of N-doped CDs was linked to the carboxyl group of the functional monomer acrylic acid using a covalent coupling approach involving 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS). This approach allowed for the facile integration of CDs into the nanoMIPs structure during the synthesis of nanoMIPs. By utilizing the solid-phase epitope imprinting method, we focused on an immunodominant epitope found on the flagellar capping protein (FliD) of C. jejuni. The fluorescent sensor based on the CDs@nanoMIPs demonstrated a meaningful sensitivity and selectivity in its diagnostic capabilities with a very low LOD. This innovative combination of fluorescent CDs and nanoMIPs promises improvements in C. jejuni detection and also creates opportunities for developing similarly sensitive, fast, reliable, and affordable sensors to detect a wide range of other pathogenic bacteria using easy-to-use and cost-efficient fluorescent sensing techniques.

Sodium citrate tribasic dihydrate, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), N,N'-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm), acrylic acid (AA), N-(3-aminopropyl) methacrylamide hydrochloride (APMA), N-tert-butylacrylamide (TBAm), N,N,N',N'-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED), ammonium persulphate (APS), ethanol (absolute), methanol (ACS reagent, ≥ 99.8 %), acetone (ACS reagent, ≥ 99.5 %), toluene (anhydrous), glutaraldehyde, ethanolamine, hydrochloric acid-phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co. (Hamburg, Germany). 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) was provided by Fisher Scientific (Schwerte, Germany). The epitope of C. jejuni (EDANGNKVNTKVMLS) was obtained from GenScript Biotech (Nanjing, China). Glass beads (0.75–1.0 mm) and syringe filters (Rotilabo PTFE, 0.45 µm and 0.22 µm) were obtained from Carl Roth (Karlsruhe, Germany). Double-distilled water (DDW) was produced by an Ion Exchange Mischbettpatrone from Arno Willers (Hamburg, Germany).

N-doped CDs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted method, due to its inherent advantages such as simplicity and speed [20]. Briefly, a mixture of sodium citrate tribasic dihydrate (2.0 g), yogurt (5.0 g), and HCl (3 M) was sonicated (Transonic Digital S, Elma Schmidbauer GmbH, Germany) and heated in a household microwave (Samsung MC28H5013AW/EG, 900 W, Suwon, South Korea) at 180 W for 10 min (Fig. 1 ). Next, the resulting brown solution, containing CDs, was combined with double distilled water (DDW, 10 mL) and stirred magnetically. After centrifuging the solution at 8,600 rpm for 20 min, the solution of CDs was purified by passing it through a 0.45 µm membrane filter.

The solid-phase synthesis method was used to synthesize CDs@nanoMIPs with glass beads as a solid support, following a previously established protocol [21] (Fig. 1). The detailed synthesis procedure is provided in the following subsections, presented sequentially within this study.

Glass beads (60 g) were activated by boiling in a NaOH solution (2 M, 40 mL) for 15 min. They were then extensively washed with DDW and PBS until the pH of the washing solution was maintained in the range of 7.0–7.5. After the DDW and PBS washing steps, the glass beads were washed with acetone and then dried under a nitrogen atmosphere. The dried glass beads were subjected to silanization by immersing them in a solution of APTES (2 % v/v) in anhydrous toluene for 24 h. The silanization process was completed with successive washes with acetone and methanol, followed by another round of drying under a nitrogen atmosphere.

The dry glass beads were treated with glutaraldehyde solution (7 % v/v in PBS) for 2 h, which introduced aldehyde functional groups on their surface. These aldehyde groups enabled the covalent binding of the template epitope. Any excess glutaraldehyde solution was subsequently removed by washing the glass beads with DDW. To immobilize the template epitope, a solution containing the epitope sequence of DGTTEDANGNKVNTK (Fig. 1, 10 mg in 40 mL PBS) was applied to the glass beads and left to incubate overnight. Before adding NaBH4 (1.0 mg mL−1), any unbound epitope was effectively removed from the glass beads through washing with DDW. To prevent the formation of the Schiff base, the glass beads were treated with a solution of NaBH4 for 30 min, and then rewashed with DDW. Afterward, the glass beads were exposed to an ethanolamine solution (0.1 mM in PBS) for 15 min, followed by another round of washing with DDW, concluding with drying under a nitrogen atmosphere.

The synthesis of CDs-incorporated nanoMIPs commenced with the preparation of the CDs monomer. This phase involved establishing a chemical bond between the carboxylic group of acrylic acid and the amino group present on the CDs, facilitated by EDC-NHS chemistry. The process began with the sonication of EDC and NHS in a PBS buffer, followed by the addition of acrylic acid. After adding acrylic acid, the carboxylic acid groups were activated through magnetic stirring for 60 min. In the next step, N-doped CDs were introduced and allowed to mix for 2 h. This phase resulted in AA bearing CDs, which were then utilized in the polymerization process (Fig. 1). Following this, a monomer solution was carefully prepared in PBS, consisting of various components: NIPAm (35.39 mol %), MBA (1.33 mol %), APMA (33.34 mol %), TBAm (26.65 mol %), and AA bearing CDs (3.29 mol %). TBAm was dissolved in ethanol before being incorporated into the pre-polymerization solution. Before combining with the glass beads, the solution was sonicated using a Transsonic Digital S instrument (Elma Schmidbauer GmbH, Singen, Germany) for 20 min. Concomitantly, the solution underwent a 20-min bubbling process with nitrogen. Following a 2-min interval of nitrogen purging, APS (60 mg mL−1 in water) and TEMED (24 μL) were introduced into the mixture. The resulting blend was purged with nitrogen for 30 s and then kept at 22 ± 2°C for 1 h to facilitate polymerization (Fig. 1).

After the polymerization process was completed, the glass beads were transferred to a solid-phase extraction (SPE) cartridge, and the supernatant was discarded. A series of steps were then taken to carefully purify the synthesized product. Initially, the glass beads underwent several washes with cold DDW, involving five cycles of 20 mL at 5 °C. This iterative process effectively removed low-affinity polymers, residual unreacted monomers, and other undesired residues. A further purification step was then performed, which involved incubating the glass beads with hot DDW, repeated seven times with 5-min intervals at a temperature of 60 °C (Fig. 1). This process enabled the isolation of high-affinity CDs@nanoMIPs. After purification, the nanoMIPs were freeze-dried, leading to their dissolution in DDW at a concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1. Due to the fundamental principle of SPE, which revolves around synthesizing MIPs around the immobilized template on a solid phase, generating non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) in the absence of a template becomes unfeasible within this framework [22]. Control nanoMIPs were synthesized with an alternate peptide sequence (DTNRSRACHPCSP) following the same outlined protocol.

The absorption properties of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs were examined using a Cary 60 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). For the assessment of fluorescence properties and photostability, a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) was employed. The hydrodynamic particle size distribution profiles and zeta potential values of the as-synthesized nanoparticles were determined using a dynamic light scattering (DLS) device (Malvern Panalytical, Zetasizer Pro, Herrenberg, Germany). To facilitate this analysis, CDs@nanoMIPs were dispersed in water by sonication. Surface functional groups on the materials were also analyzed using a Carry 630 FT-IR device (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). The crystalline properties of the nanoparticles were investigated using a Rigaku SmartLab 9 K X-ray diffractometer, which was equipped with CuKα radiation at a wavelength of λ = 1.540 Å. To determine the chemical composition of the sample surface, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed with an XPS UHV system (PREVAC sp. zoo, Rogow, Poland). This system featured an Al anode operated at 300 W. In this analysis, survey scans were conducted with three iterations and a pass energy of 200 eV, while high-resolution scans involved 20 iterations and a pass energy of 50 eV. CasaXPS software, version 2.3.23, was used to analyze the XPS spectra. The Shirley algorithm was employed to quantify the background of each spectrum. Moreover, Gaussian-Lorentzian model peaks were used to fit all relevant photoemission peaks. Charge correction was applied by fitting the C 1 s main peak and adjusting other spectra accordingly, with the peak position set at 284.8 eV.

The quantum yield (QY) of N-doped CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference standard (QY = 0.54; Eq. (1). To ensure precision in the measurements, the absorbances of both N-doped CDs and quinine sulfate were maintained in the range of 0.1 to 0.01.
(1)
QY =
QY
S
I C
I S
A S
A C
n
c
2
n
s
2
where I is the integrated emission intensity, A is the optical density, and n is the refractive index. The subscripts “s” refers to the reference standard, with refractive indices represented as n c and n s, both equal to 1.33 [23].

The morphology of CDs@nanoMIPs was thoroughly characterized using two distinct techniques. First, high-contrast transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted with an FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit BioTwin CTEM operating at 120 kV. For TEM analysis, a sample droplet was deposited on a carbon-coated copper grid and allowed to air dry following the sonication for 10 min. The second technique involved fluorescence microscopy (BZ-X; Keyence, Neu-Isenburg, Germany). Images of the dried CDs@nanoMIPs sample on a glass slide were captured, and a blue fluorescence filter was applied to utilize their fluorescence properties.

For C. jejuni (ATCC 33560), the microorganism was cultured on Karmali Agar (Thermo Scientific) and then inactivated through heat treatment in a water bath at 70 °C for 30 min. For Escherichia coli (ATCC 25992) or Salmonella typhimurium (ATCC 14028), a single colony was inoculated into 5 mL of medium and allowed to incubate at 37 °C. After a 12-h incubation, the optical density (OD) of the liquid medium was determined at a wavelength of 600 nm. The suspension was adjusted to contain 1 × 1010 bacterial cells per mL based on the measured OD value. The bacterial cells were inactivated by subjecting them to an incubation temperature of 70 °C for 30 min. The effectiveness of the inactivation process was confirmed by cultivating the bacteria on agar plates and in a liquid culture medium for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensor development for C. Jejuni detection
ce:section.ce:para: The detection assays were carefully executed utilizing a Fluoromax 4 spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Longjumeau, France). To initiate the process, the target CDs@nanoMIPs (0.1 mg) were dispersed in PBS solution (3.0 mL, pH 7.0). The fluorescence values of the blank solution were measured under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, employing a 5 nm slit. Following this, different volumes of the target epitope/C. jejuni, covering a concentration range from 5 to 500 nM for the target epitope and from 1 × 101 to 1 × 109 CFU mL−1 for C. jejuni, were introduced into the cuvette. Mixing was performed by gently flipping the cuvette 5–6 times. Fluorescence measurements were recorded following a 5-min incubation for the target epitope and 2 min for C. jejuni. The fluorescence values obtained at the maximum emission wavelength were designated as F0 (in the absence of the epitope/bacteria) and F1 (in the presence of the epitope/bacteria). The degree of fluorescence enhancement was calculated using the formula F1-F0. To determine the LOD, the formula 3σ/K was employed, with σ representing the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 20) and K signifying the slope of the calibration plot [24]. Furthermore, the binding capacity of the control CDs@nanoMIPs was evaluated using the same protocol directed at both the target epitope and C. jejuni.

To evaluate the sensor's specificity using CDs@nanoMIPs, experiments were conducted with two additional bacterial strains: E. coli (ATCC 25992) and S. typhimurium (ATCC14028). Solutions containing these bacterial strains were prepared at concentrations identical to those used for C. jejuni (1 × 101 ––1 × 109 CFU mL−1). The sensor's specificity was systematically examined for each concentration of these two bacterial strains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of C. Jejuni in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water was chosen to analyze C. jejuni in a real sample, simulating a real-world contamination scenario. Serial dilutions of C. jejuni were introduced by spiking a suspension of C. jejuni into the tap water. Fluorescence analysis of the contaminated tap water samples was then carried out using our established detection assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of specific epitope for C. jejuni
ce:section.ce:para: C. jejuni, classified as a Gram-negative bacterium, is characterized by its unique helical shape and polar-flagellated morphology. This polar flagellation gives C. jejuni a distinct advantage in terms of rapid movement through aqueous environments, surpassing the mobility of various other bacteria, including S. enteritidis, E. coli, and Vibrio cholerae, even in highly viscous surroundings. This attribute plays a pivotal role in host colonization and contributes to its virulence [25]. Therefore, the polar flagellum of C. jejuni is acknowledged as a crucial virulence factor, essential for its mobility, adhesion, host colonization, and invasion [2,5]. The antigenic FliD is notably abundant among various strains and isolates of C. jejuni, [26] making it a suitable candidate for the identification of this bacterium. In a separate study, a comprehensive investigation identified three linear epitopes on the FliD protein with significant immunoreactivity. These epitopes were classified as immunodominant due to their consistent reactivity and limited variability across 64 chicken sera [27]. Out of these epitopes, one hydrophilic epitope (FliD 91) was selected as the template for the current study. The solid-phase epitope imprinting method was used to imprint this hydrophilic epitope for the first time, resulting in the creation of fluorescent nanoMIPs (Fig. 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-doped CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped CDs were synthesized using the microwave-assisted method, with citric acid and yogurt serving as precursors (QY = 1.1). The introduction of nitrogen groups into the CDs, facilitated by yogurt [23], allowed for the subsequent binding of CDs to acrylic acid through EDC-NHS chemistry (Fig. 1). The template epitope utilized in this study consisted of a combination of acidic (20 %), basic (13.3 %), polar (53.33 %), and hydrophobic (13.33 %) amino acids. The synthesis of CDs@nanoMIPs was accomplished by employing NIPAm (as the backbone), MBA (as the cross-linker), APMA (as the positive monomer), and TBAm (as the hydrophobic monomer), along with acrylic acid-bearing CDs. This combination of components underwent a 1-h polymerization process (Fig. 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 A displays the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of both CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs. Both exhibited significant ultraviolet light absorption, extending into the visible range. In the CDs spectrum, two distinct peaks were evident at 279 nm and 214 nm, corresponding to π → π* and n → π* transitions related to the C = C bond. The n − π* transition can be ascribed to the C = O or NH2 bond. Under 365 nm excitation, both CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs emitted blue fluorescence (Fig. 2A). As shown in Fig. 2B, the emission spectra for CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs are similar. The maximum emission was observed at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm for the CDs (Fig. 2C) which showed excitation-dependent emission properties (Fig. 2D). Therefore, an excitation wavelength of 345 nm was selected for all fluorescence measurements (Fig. 2E). Similar to CDs, the emission intensities of CDs@nanoMIPs exhibited an increase as their concentrations increased (Fig. 2F). Interestingly, CDs@nanoMIPs demonstrated photostability on par with that of CDs (Fig. 2G). These results collectively indicate that the incorporation of CDs into nanoMIPs did not compromise the optical properties of CDs. Consequently, the innovative methodology successfully achieved the synthesis of fluorescent and photostable CDs@nanoMIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and size of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of CDs is illustrated in Fig. 3 A. The presence of 2θ values at 25.18 (d = 0.320), corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (with a theoretical 2θ value of 26.42 and a d-spacing of 0.336), suggests the presence of a moderate graphitic structure. It is plausible that the N-doping process has contributed to some degree of disruption in the graphitic structure, as previously reported [28]. The average hydrodynamic size of the CDs was calculated to be 13.84 nm (Fig. 3B). Furthermore, when observing the image of CDs@nanoMIPs through fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 3C), the nanoparticles exhibit a distinctive fluorescent blue dot-like appearance. The TEM image of CDs@nanoMIPs offers insights into their morphology and size distribution (Fig. 2H). These nanoparticles exhibit a moderately polydisperse and spherical shape, with an average size of 59 nm. It is important to note that the average hydrodynamic size of CDs@nanoMIPs, determined as 186.1 nm using DLS (Fig. 3C), appears relatively larger. This size variation is attributed to their tendency to swell and agglomerate, a common phenomenon observed with such nanoparticles [29,30]. Furthermore, the polydispersity index of these nanoparticles (PDI = 0.3) confirms their high monodispersity, consistent with the observations from the TEM image. The zeta potential values of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs were determined to be −17.38 mV and −16.83 mV, respectively (Fig. 3D). These results indicate that the incorporation of CDs into nanoMIPs did not induce significant alterations in the zeta potential of the resulting nanoMIPs. This observation aligns with findings reported by Zhang et al. [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface functional groups of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Surface functional groups and elemental composition of both CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs were analyzed through XPS. The XPS spectra and elemental content of CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs are depicted in Fig. 4 A-C. The presence of characteristic peaks for C, N, O, and Na in both CDs and CDs@nanoMIPs was evidenced (Fig. 4C).

CDs@nanoMIPs displayed higher carbon and nitrogen content, along with lower oxygen content when compared to CDs. The sodium content in CDs@nanoMIPs (3.98 %) suggests that they were encapsulated with CDs at an approximate ratio of 19 %. XPS analysis of the C1s spectra revealed the presence of three distinct types of carbon atoms in both samples: C-C/C = C, C-O/C-N, and C = O, each with varying ratios (Fig. 4A and 4B). The FT-IR spectra of CDs, CDs@nanoMIPs, and the template epitope are also depicted in Fig. 4D. While CDs@nanoMIPs compared to CDs exhibited a distinct FT-IR spectrum and displayed a resemblance to the spectrum of the template epitope. The heightened intensity of C = C bonds in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs@nanoMIPs was consistent with the observations in the XPS spectrum. The same functional groups identified in CDs via XPS analysis were also evident in the FT-IR results.

The detection mechanism of the developed method relies on the observed increase in fluorescence emission intensity when CDs@nanoMIPs interact with C. jejuni, as depicted in Fig. 5 A. To thoroughly assess the performance characteristics of the developed fluorescent sensor employing CDs@nanoMIPs, investigations were conducted for both the target epitope and C. jejuni. When detecting the target epitope, a specific low concentration of CDs@nanoMIPs was subjected to varying concentrations of the target epitope (5–500 nM) for 5 min. After this interaction, changes in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@nanoMIPs were monitored, and the detection outcomes were derived from the observed fluorescence enhancement values. The fluorescent sensor utilizing CDs@nanoMIPs effectively identified the target epitope and displayed a linear response (R2 = 0.98) with increasing concentrations (Fig. 5B).

Similarly, the detection of C. jejuni was conducted using a procedure akin to that employed for the target epitope. The calibration curve depicted in Fig. 5C illustrates that the sensor's responses displayed a linear relationship with C. jejuni bacterial concentrations, presented on a logarithmic concentration scale. These results collectively signify the effective detection capability of the developed sensor for C. jejuni, with a calculated LOD of 4.6 CFU mL−1.

The dissociation constant (KD) for C. jejuni and the target epitope was determined by fitting the sensor data to three primary binding isotherms: Langmuir, Freundlich, and Langmuir-Freundlich models (Table S1) [21]. For the target epitope, the Langmuir model —with the highest R2 of 0.972 and an m value of 1.0 —indicates that CDs@nanoMIPs bind to a homogenous surface, reflecting the small and well-defined nature of the target epitope. In contrast, the Freundlich model shows the best fit for C. jejuni, with an R2 value of 0.990 and an m value of 0.464, suggesting heterogeneous binding sites. This result is consistent with C. jejuni's complex and variable surface, which provides multiple binding sites with different affinities on the sensor surface. The very low KD value (0.04 CFU mL−1) reflects a strong binding affinity of CDs@nanoMIPs (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the proposed fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Control- CDs@nanoMIPs was produced to evaluate the selectivity of the fabricated sensor. This was achieved by imprinting a distinct template peptide (DTNRSRACHPCSP) under the same synthesis conditions, and fluorescence studies were then conducted using the same assay as previously described for C. jejuni detection (Fig. 5D). The developed sensor exhibited significantly higher affinities for both the target epitope and C. jejuni compared to the control CDs@nanoMIPs, across all concentrations of the analytes. The selectivity coefficient, denoted as “k”, was calculated as follows (Eq. (2):
(2)
k = Δ
F
CDs @ n a n o M I P s
/ Δ
F
control - C D s @ n a n o M I P

where ΔF represents the fluorescence enhancement value measured for CDs@nanoMIPs and control ones, respectively. The selectivity coefficient “k” is a quantitative measure of the sensor's preference for the target analyte over the control analyte.

The average ‘k’ value was calculated as 6.45, derived from a range of values (9.61 to 1.47) for the epitope and a value of 5.5 for C. jejuni, within the range of 9.87 to 1.80. The significantly higher average selectivity coefficient values obtained for the sensor underscore the enhanced and specific recognition of the target epitope and bacteria by CDs@nanoMIPs. This recognition capability can be attributed to the imprinting process, which imparts the sensor with the template's shape and surface functionalities, as demonstrated in previous studies [21,29]. Interestingly, it is worth noting that the selectivity coefficient values exhibit a decreasing trend at higher concentrations, which in contrast to the values observed at lower concentrations. This phenomenon may be attributed to non-specific interactions that may occur at elevated analyte concentrations, leading to a decrease in the sensor's specificity. This observation aligns with the findings of another study [29], emphasizing the complexity of sensor-analyte interactions and the importance of interpreting data carefully across various concentration ranges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the proposed fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor response to E. coli (ATCC 25992) and S. typhimurium (ATCC14028), both of which are Gram-negative bacteria with peritrichous flagella [31], was examined. Specificity tests were performed at different concentrations (1 × 101 – 1 × 108 CFU mL−1, Fig. 5E). The developed sensor identified C. jejuni much better than E. coli (ATCC 25992) or S. typhimurium (ATCC14028) at all investigated concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of C. Jejuni in tap water
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water was chosen as a representative real sample because of the prevalence of C. jejuni infections from sources like chicken, raw milk, and untreated water [32]. Tap water samples were spiked with different concentrations of C. jejuni (1 × 101-1 × 109 CFU mL−1) and used without prior treatment. Each tap water sample was analyzed following the protocol outlined in Section 2.8. The sensor showed no measurable response at low bacterial concentrations (1 × 10 – 1 × 103 CFU mL−1). However, it exhibited a linear detection range at higher concentrations (1 × 104 – 1 × 109 CFU mL−1). The calibration curve derived from these results (Fig. 5F) confirms the sensor's capability to detect C. jejuni contamination in tap water with high precision (R2 = 0.98). These findings validate the successful detection of C. jejuni by the developed fluorescent sensor in real water samples.

The CDs@nanoMIPs-based fluorescent biosensor exhibits exceptional sensitivity for detecting C. jejuni, with rapid response times, cost-effectiveness, and a very low LOD of 16 CFU mL−1, enabling detection even at minimal concentrations in environmental samples. Besides, the analytical versatility of nanoMIPs supports diverse methodological applications. Altogether, these advantages make this biosensor highly promising for food safety and environmental monitoring. As shown in Table S2, the CDs@nanoMIPs-based sensor compared to other biosensors stands out as a sensitive, efficient, and cost-effective solution for C. jejuni detection.

This study presented an innovative approach for the detection of C. jejuni, a primary causative agent of foodborne gastroenteritis, by introducing a novel fluorescence-based bacterial sensor employing CDs@nanoMIPs receptors. Importantly, this research represented the first instance of utilizing fluorescent synthetic receptors designed for the specific detection of C. jejuni. The synthesis of nanoMIPs was accomplished through a solid phase epitope imprinting technique, utilizing an immunodominant epitope derived from the FliD protein located on C. jejuni's polar flagella. This new technique could profoundly enhance the identification and monitoring of C. jejuni, addressing crucial challenges in the realms of food safety and public health. The incorporation of N-doped CDs as a fluorescent tag into nanoMIPs signified a significant advancement, yielding an entirely synthetic and cost-effective receptor. This integration was accomplished by employing a state-of-the-art technique that entailed the coupling of N-CDs with acrylic acid as a functional monomer. Next, it led to the successful development of blue-fluorescent and photostable CDs@nanoMIPs, with a high encapsulation efficiency of around 19%. This innovative method provided a robust platform to produce highly functional and efficient synthetic receptors with diverse applications. The amalgamation of nanoMIPs and CDs gave rise to a highly sensitive and selective detection mechanism for C. jejuni, relying on fluorescence enhancement within a linear range of 1 × 101 – 1 × 108 CFU mL−1 (R2 = 0.99) and a very low LOD of 16 CFU mL−1. Furthermore, the developed fluorescent sensor demonstrated its efficacy in detecting C. jejuni in water, showing a linear range of 1 × 104 – 1 × 109 CFU mL−1 (R2 = 0.98). By incorporating the synthetic receptor of CDs@nanoMIPs and fluorescent biorecognition element, this study can realize a novel pathway to enhance detection strategies targeting pathogenic microorganisms, thus signifying this work as a transformative proof-of-concept endeavor.

Saliha Dinç: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tim Tjardts: Methodology, Investigation. Gregor Maschkowitz: Methodology, Investigation. Vivian Lukaszczuk: Methodology, Investigation. Seyed Mohammad Taghi Gharibzahedi: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Zeynep Altintas: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support provided by the German Research Foundation (DFG, Grant number: 428780268) and the Aventis Foundation (Grant number: 80304368). Also, the authors would like to appreciate Justin Jetter (M.Sc., Institute of Materials Science, Kiel University) for conducting XRD analysis and the Scientific and Technical Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK) for providing a scholarship (grant number: 1059B192000595).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112301.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
